content:
# Numbers Chapter 24 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 177-178 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0131-0132 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_024_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_024_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Spirit of God Upon Balaam (vv. 1-2)

**1** And Balaam saw that it was good in the eyes of YHWH to bless Israel, and he did not go as at other times to seek enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness.

**2** And Balaam lifted up his eyes and saw Israel dwelling according to its tribes. And the Spirit of God came upon him.

---

### Balaam's Third Oracle (vv. 3-9)

**3** And he took up his discourse and said:

"The utterance of Balaam the son of Beor,
and the utterance of the man whose eye is opened,

**4** the utterance of the one who hears the words of God,
who sees the vision of the Almighty,
falling down yet having his eyes uncovered:

**5** How fair are your tents, O Jacob,
your dwelling places, O Israel!

**6** Like valleys that stretch out,
like gardens beside a river,
like aloes that YHWH has planted,
like cedars beside the waters.

**7** Water shall flow from his buckets,
and his seed shall be in many waters.
And his king shall be higher than Agag,
and his kingdom shall be exalted.

**8** God, who brought him out from Egypt,
is for him like the horns of the wild ox.
He shall devour the nations, his adversaries,
and their bones he shall break,
and with his arrows he shall pierce them.

**9** He crouches, he lies down as a lion,
and as a lioness — who shall rouse him?
Blessed are those who bless you,
and cursed are those who curse you."

---

### Balak's Anger and Balaam's Response (vv. 10-14)

**10** And the anger of Balak was kindled against Balaam, and he struck his hands together. And Balak said to Balaam, "To curse my enemies I called you, and behold, you have surely blessed them these three times!

**11** And now, flee to your place! I said I would greatly honor you, but behold, YHWH has held you back from honor."

**12** And Balaam said to Balak, "Did I not also speak to your messengers whom you sent to me, saying,

**13** 'If Balak were to give me his house full of silver and gold, I could not transgress the mouth of YHWH to do good or evil from my own heart. What YHWH speaks, that I shall speak'?

**14** And now, behold, I am going to my people. Come, I will advise you what this people will do to your people in the latter days."

---

### Balaam's Fourth Oracle — The Star and the Scepter (vv. 15-19)

**15** And he took up his discourse and said:

"The utterance of Balaam the son of Beor,
and the utterance of the man whose eye is opened,

**16** the utterance of the one who hears the words of God,
and who knows the knowledge of the Most High,
who sees the vision of the Almighty,
falling down yet having his eyes uncovered:

**17** I see him, but not now;
I behold him, but not near.
A star shall come forth out of Jacob,
and a scepter shall rise out of Israel.
And it shall crush the forehead of Moab
and tear down all the sons of Seth.

**18** And Edom shall be a possession,
and Seir shall be a possession — his enemies —
while Israel does valiantly.

**19** And one from Jacob shall exercise dominion,
and he shall destroy the survivor from the city."

---

### Oracles Against the Nations (vv. 20-24)

**20** And he saw Amalek, and he took up his discourse and said:

"First among the nations is Amalek,
but his end shall be unto destruction."

**21** And he saw the Kenite, and he took up his discourse and said:

"Enduring is your dwelling place,
and set in the rock is your nest.

**22** Nevertheless, Cain shall be for burning.
How long? Asshur shall carry you away captive."

**23** And he took up his discourse and said:

"Alas, who shall live when God does this?

**24** And ships shall come from the coast of Kittim,
and they shall afflict Asshur and they shall afflict Eber,
and he also shall be unto destruction."

---

### Departure (v. 25)

**25** And Balaam rose and went and returned to his place. And Balak also went his way.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **v. 1 -- "he did not go as at other times to seek enchantments" (לֹא־הָלַךְ כְּפַעַם־בְּפַעַם לִקְרַאת נְחָשִׁים):** The noun נְחָשִׁים ("enchantments, omens") is the plural of נַחַשׁ, the very practice declared ineffective against Israel in 23:23. The phrase כְּפַעַם־בְּפַעַם ("as time after time") is an idiom meaning "as on previous occasions." The Leningrad Codex reads this clearly. Balaam's behavioral shift is theologically significant: he abandons divination and opens himself directly to divine inspiration. The LXX renders the enchantments as οἰωνούς ("auguries, bird-omens").

2. **v. 1 -- "he set his face toward the wilderness" (וַיָּשֶׁת אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּר פָּנָיו):** The verb שִׁית ("to set, place") with פָּנִים ("face") indicates deliberate orientation. Balaam turns toward the wilderness where Israel is encamped rather than seeking a remote location for omen-reading. The LXX renders ἀπέστρεψεν τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ εἰς τὴν ἔρημον ("he turned his face toward the wilderness").

3. **v. 2 -- "the Spirit of God came upon him" (וַתְּהִי עָלָיו רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים):** This is the first time in the Balaam narrative that the רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים ("Spirit of God") is said to come upon him. In chapters 22-23, YHWH "put a word in his mouth"; now the mode of inspiration intensifies from verbal communication to spiritual possession. The LXX renders πνεῦμα θεοῦ ("Spirit of God"), a direct equivalent.

4. **v. 3 -- "the man whose eye is opened" (הַגֶּבֶר שְׁתֻם הָעָיִן):** The word שְׁתֻם is notoriously difficult. It may mean "opened" (from a root meaning "to uncover") or "closed" (from a root meaning "to stop up"). The Masoretic pointing as a passive participle (שְׁתֻם) is ambiguous. Most ancient versions, including the LXX (ὁ ἀληθινῶς ὁρῶν, "the one truly seeing"), support "opened." The formal equivalence follows the "opened" interpretation, which fits the context of visionary revelation. The phrase recurs verbatim in v. 15.

5. **v. 4 -- "falling down yet having his eyes uncovered" (נֹפֵל וּגְלוּי עֵינָיִם):** The participle נֹפֵל ("falling") likely describes an ecstatic trance state in which the seer collapses while his inner vision remains active. The adjective גְּלוּי ("uncovered, revealed") modifying עֵינָיִם ("eyes") indicates spiritual sight during physical prostration. The LXX renders ὅστις ἐν ὕπνῳ with reference to sleep-vision, interpreting the trance differently.

6. **v. 5 -- "How fair are your tents, O Jacob" (מַה־טֹּבוּ אֹהָלֶיךָ יַעֲקֹב):** This verse became one of the most beloved liturgical lines in Jewish tradition (the prayer "Mah Tovu"). The interrogative מַה here functions as an exclamatory "how!" The parallelism of "tents" (אֹהָלִים) with "dwelling places" (מִשְׁכָּנוֹת) may refer to the physical encampment or, in later liturgical usage, to synagogues and houses of study.

7. **v. 6 -- "Like valleys that stretch out" (כִּנְחָלִים נִטָּיוּ):** The noun נְחָלִים means "wadis, valleys, torrents" -- watercourses that in the Levantine landscape represent fertility and life. The verb נָטָה ("to stretch out, extend") in the Niphal depicts the valleys spreading across the landscape. The LXX renders ὡσεὶ νάπαι σκιάζουσαι ("like shady glens"), interpreting the image slightly differently. The fourfold simile (valleys, gardens, aloes, cedars) paints Israel's encampment as a verdant paradise.

8. **v. 6 -- "like aloes that YHWH has planted" (כַּאֲהָלִים נָטַע יְהוָה):** The אֲהָלִים are aromatic aloe trees (Aquilaria species), prized for their fragrance. The attribution of planting to YHWH elevates the image from natural beauty to divine cultivation. The LXX renders ὡσεὶ σκηναί ("like tents"), apparently reading אֹהָלִים ("tents") for אֲהָלִים ("aloes") -- a reading facilitated by the near-identical spelling in unpointed text.

9. **v. 7 -- "Water shall flow from his buckets" (יִזַּל־מַיִם מִדָּלְיָו):** The imagery of overflowing water symbolizes abundance and prosperity. The דְּלִי (dual: דָּלְיָו, "his two buckets") evokes the agricultural image of irrigation. The LXX renders ἐξελεύσεται ἄνθρωπος ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος αὐτοῦ ("a man shall come forth from his seed"), a markedly different and messianic reading that diverges from the MT's water imagery.

10. **v. 7 -- "his king shall be higher than Agag" (וְיָרֹם מֵאֲגַג מַלְכּוֹ):** Agag is the title or name of the Amalekite king (cf. 1 Samuel 15). The LXX reads Γωγ (Gog) for Agag, an eschatological substitution that connects this oracle to the Gog traditions of Ezekiel 38-39. The Masoretic reading "Agag" is historically more specific and likely original. The verb רוּם ("to be high, exalted") in the Qal indicates superiority.

11. **v. 8 -- "their bones he shall break, and with his arrows he shall pierce them" (וְעַצְמֹתֵיהֶם יְגָרֵם וְחִצָּיו יִמְחָץ):** The verb גָּרַם means "to gnaw, crush bones." The verb מָחַץ means "to shatter, pierce." The imagery is of total military destruction. The LXX renders with similar violence, using ἐκμυελιεῖ ("he shall extract the marrow") for "break bones," intensifying the image.

12. **v. 9 -- "He crouches, he lies down as a lion" (כָּרַע שָׁכַב כַּאֲרִי):** This echoes Genesis 49:9 (Jacob's blessing of Judah) almost verbatim: "He crouches, he lies down as a lion, and as a lioness -- who shall rouse him?" The literary allusion connects Balaam's oracle to the patriarchal blessings. The verb כָּרַע ("to crouch, bow down") and שָׁכַב ("to lie down") depict the lion at rest, dangerous even in repose.

13. **v. 9 -- "Blessed are those who bless you, and cursed are those who curse you" (מְבָרְכֶיךָ בָרוּךְ וְאֹרְרֶיךָ אָרוּר):** This directly echoes the Abrahamic covenant formula of Genesis 12:3 and 27:29. The participles מְבָרְכֶיךָ ("those blessing you") and אֹרְרֶיךָ ("those cursing you") with the passive adjectives בָּרוּךְ ("blessed") and אָרוּר ("cursed") create a chiastic structure. Balaam, hired to curse, now pronounces the curse upon those who curse Israel -- effectively upon his own employer.

14. **v. 10 -- "he struck his hands together" (וַיִּסְפֹּק אֶת־כַּפָּיו):** The verb סָפַק ("to clap, strike together") with כַּפַּיִם ("palms, hands") is a gesture of angry frustration (cf. Job 27:23; Lamentations 2:15). The LXX renders συνεκρότησεν τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ("he clapped his hands together"), preserving the gesture.

15. **v. 10 -- "you have surely blessed them" (בָּרֵךְ בֵּרַכְתָּ):** Infinitive absolute construction, rendered with "surely" per binding conventions. The addition of "these three times" (זֶה שָׁלֹשׁ פְּעָמִים) echoes the three-times motif from the donkey episode (22:28, 32-33), creating a structural parallel: the donkey saw the angel three times, and Balaam blessed three times.

16. **v. 11 -- "I would greatly honor you" (כַּבֵּד אֲכַבֶּדְךָ):** Infinitive absolute construction: כַּבֵּד אֲכַבֶּדְךָ, literally "honoring I would honor you." Rendered with "greatly honor" to convey the emphatic force. The verb כָּבֵד ("to be heavy, honored") in the Piel means "to honor, treat with respect." Balak's statement implies financial reward and social prestige.

17. **v. 11 -- "YHWH has held you back from honor" (מְנָעֲךָ יְהוָה מִכָּבוֹד):** The verb מָנַע ("to withhold, hold back, restrain") with מִן ("from") plus כָּבוֹד ("honor, glory") constitutes Balak's theological interpretation: YHWH is responsible for Balaam's loss of reward. Ironically, Balak now acknowledges YHWH's sovereignty even in his anger.

18. **v. 13 -- "the mouth of YHWH" (אֶת־פִּי יְהוָה):** The phrase פִּי יְהוָה ("mouth of YHWH") is a metonym for the divine command or decree. Balaam reiterates the principle established in 22:18 that he is bound by YHWH's word. The preposition אֶת here marks the direct object governed by the infinitive לַעֲבֹר ("to transgress, cross over").

19. **v. 14 -- "in the latter days" (בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים):** The phrase אַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים is a terminus technicus in prophetic literature, denoting the eschatological future or "the end of days." It signals that the fourth oracle will shift from present description to future prophecy. The LXX renders ἐπ᾿ ἐσχάτου τῶν ἡμερῶν ("at the last of the days"), preserving the eschatological orientation.

20. **v. 16 -- "who knows the knowledge of the Most High" (וְיֹדֵעַ דַּעַת עֶלְיוֹן):** This line is absent from the third oracle's introduction (v. 3-4) and is unique to the fourth oracle, reflecting its heightened revelatory status. The divine name עֶלְיוֹן ("Most High") is an archaic title for God emphasizing cosmic sovereignty. The LXX renders ἐπιστάμενος ἐπιστήμην παρὰ ὑψίστου ("knowing knowledge from the Most High").

21. **v. 17 -- "A star shall come forth out of Jacob, and a scepter shall rise out of Israel" (דָּרַךְ כּוֹכָב מִיַּעֲקֹב וְקָם שֵׁבֶט מִיִּשְׂרָאֵל):** This is the theological apex of the entire Balaam cycle. The verb דָּרַךְ ("to tread, march") applied to a star (כּוֹכָב) creates the image of a celestial body striding across the heavens. The שֵׁבֶט ("scepter, rod, tribe") is a royal symbol. Together they constitute a messianic prophecy. The LXX renders ἀνατελεῖ ἄστρον ἐξ Ιακωβ ("a star shall rise from Jacob"), and ἀναστήσεται ἄνθρωπος ἐξ Ισραηλ ("a man shall rise from Israel"), making the messianic identification explicit. This verse was central to the Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135 CE), where Rabbi Akiva identified Simon bar Kokhba ("son of the star") as the fulfillment of this oracle.

22. **v. 17 -- "shall crush the forehead of Moab" (וּמָחַץ פַּאֲתֵי מוֹאָב):** The verb מָחַץ ("to smite, crush, shatter") is a violent term. The noun פֵּאָה means "side, corner, forehead, temple." The phrase פַּאֲתֵי מוֹאָב ("the forehead/sides of Moab") may denote the borders of Moab or, more viscerally, the skull/temples of Moab personified. The formal equivalence renders "forehead" to preserve the bodily image.

23. **v. 17 -- "all the sons of Seth" (כָּל־בְּנֵי־שֵׁת):** The identity of "Seth" (שֵׁת) is debated. It may refer to: (a) Seth, the third son of Adam, making "sons of Seth" a universal designation for all humanity; (b) a Moabite territory or tribal name; or (c) a term derived from שָׁאָה ("tumult, devastation"), meaning "sons of tumult/destruction." The Leningrad Codex reading is שֵׁת. The LXX renders υἱοὺς Σηθ. The verb קַרְקַר ("to tear down, demolish") is a rare pilpel form of קור/קרר, intensifying the destructive imagery.

24. **v. 18 -- "And Edom shall be a possession" (וְהָיָה אֱדוֹם יְרֵשָׁה):** The noun יְרֵשָׁה ("a possession, an inheritance") is from the root יָרַשׁ ("to possess, inherit, dispossess"). The parallelism of Edom with Seir (its geographic equivalent) emphasizes totality. This was historically fulfilled in David's conquest of Edom (2 Samuel 8:14). The phrase "his enemies" (אֹיְבָיו) is in apposition to Seir, clarifying that the conquered territory belongs to Israel's enemies.

25. **v. 19 -- "and he shall destroy the survivor from the city" (וְהֶאֱבִיד שָׂרִיד מֵעִיר):** The verb אָבַד in the Hiphil ("to destroy, cause to perish") combined with שָׂרִיד ("survivor, remnant") and מֵעִיר ("from a/the city") depicts the completeness of the conquest. The LXX renders ἀπολεῖ σῳζόμενον ἐκ πόλεως ("he shall destroy the one being saved from a city").

26. **v. 20 -- "First among the nations is Amalek" (רֵאשִׁית גּוֹיִם עֲמָלֵק):** The noun רֵאשִׁית ("first, beginning, chief") may mean "first in time" (the earliest to attack Israel, cf. Exodus 17:8-16) or "first in power" among the nations. The contrast with "his end shall be unto destruction" (וְאַחֲרִיתוֹ עֲדֵי אֹבֵד) creates an ironic reversal: the first shall be the last, the greatest shall perish.

27. **v. 21 -- "Enduring is your dwelling place, and set in the rock is your nest" (אֵיתָן מוֹשָׁבֶךָ וְשִׂים בַּסֶּלַע קִנֶּךָ):** The Kenites (קֵינִי) are associated with Jethro/Moses' father-in-law (Judges 1:16). The adjective אֵיתָן ("enduring, perennial, strong") describes their habitation. The wordplay on קֵינִי ("Kenite") and קֵן ("nest") is preserved in the translation. "Set in the rock" (בַּסֶּלַע) refers to their cliff-dwelling habitations (cf. the Nabatean settlement patterns).

28. **v. 22 -- "Cain shall be for burning" (כִּי אִם־יִהְיֶה לְבָעֵר קָיִן):** The name קַיִן ("Cain") is a play on the tribal name קֵינִי ("Kenite"). The verb בָּעַר means "to burn, consume." The question "How long?" (עַד־מָה) introduces temporal uncertainty before the next clause. The phrase is syntactically difficult; some render כִּי אִם as "nevertheless" or "surely." The LXX interprets this passage differently, reading the Kenite oracle as a conditional.

29. **v. 22 -- "Asshur shall carry you away captive" (אַשּׁוּר תִּשְׁבֶּךָּ):** The verb שָׁבָה ("to take captive, carry away") with Asshur (Assyria) as subject points to the Assyrian deportations as the agent of Kenite destruction. This is one of the most historically specific predictions in the Balaam oracles.

30. **v. 23 -- "Alas, who shall live when God does this?" (אוֹי מִי יִחְיֶה מִשֻּׂמוֹ אֵל):** The interjection אוֹי ("alas, woe") marks a shift to lament. The phrase מִשֻּׂמוֹ אֵל is difficult: it may mean "when God appoints/establishes this" (from שׂוּם, "to set, appoint") or "from the north of God" or other readings. The Leningrad Codex supports the reading with שׂוּם. The LXX renders ὅταν θῇ ταῦτα ὁ θεός ("when God establishes these things"), supporting the "appoint" interpretation.

31. **v. 24 -- "ships from the coast of Kittim" (וְצִים מִיַּד כִּתִּים):** The noun צִים means "ships" (a rare word, cf. Isaiah 33:21; Daniel 11:30). כִּתִּים (Kittim) originally referred to Cyprus (from the city Kition) but later became a general term for western maritime powers (in the Dead Sea Scrolls, it designates Rome). The preposition מִיַּד literally means "from the hand/side of," here functioning as "from the coast/direction of." The LXX renders ἀπὸ χειρὸς Κιτιαίων ("from the hand of the Kittians").

32. **v. 24 -- "they shall afflict Asshur and they shall afflict Eber" (וְעִנּוּ אַשּׁוּר וְעִנּוּ עֵבֶר):** The verb עָנָה in the Piel ("to afflict, oppress") is repeated for both Asshur (Assyria/Mesopotamia) and Eber (עֵבֶר). The identity of "Eber" is debated: it may refer to the ancestor of the Hebrews (Genesis 10:21), to a Transjordanian region, or to the Hebrews themselves. If the latter, the oracle predicts affliction even for Israel -- a somber conclusion to the prophetic sequence.

33. **v. 25 -- "And Balaam rose and went and returned to his place" (וַיָּקָם בִּלְעָם וַיֵּלֶךְ וַיָּשָׁב לִמְקֹמוֹ):** The three verbs in rapid succession (rose, went, returned) create a sense of abrupt departure. "To his place" (לִמְקֹמוֹ) means to his homeland. However, Numbers 31:8 records that Balaam was later killed by the Sons of Israel in the war against Midian, suggesting that his "return" was not permanent. "And Balak also went his way" (וְגַם־בָּלָק הָלַךְ לְדַרְכּוֹ) brings closure to the encounter with a note of finality -- each party departs, the oracles complete.

### Cross-References to LXX (Vaticanus)

- **v. 1:** The LXX agrees with the MT that Balaam abandoned his previous divinatory methods. Vaticanus renders the "enchantments" as οἰωνούς ("auguries"), a term specifically denoting bird-omen reading.
- **v. 2:** Both MT and LXX record the coming of the Spirit of God upon Balaam, with the LXX rendering πνεῦμα θεοῦ as a direct equivalent of רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים.
- **v. 3-4:** The LXX renders שְׁתֻם הָעָיִן as ὁ ἀληθινῶς ὁρῶν ("the one truly seeing"), resolving the Hebrew ambiguity in favor of "opened" rather than "closed."
- **v. 6:** The LXX reads "tents" (σκηναί) for "aloes" (אֲהָלִים), reflecting a well-known variant caused by the graphically identical consonantal text (אהלים can be read as either word).
- **v. 7:** The most significant divergence: where the MT reads "water shall flow from his buckets," the LXX reads "a man shall come forth from his seed" (ἐξελεύσεται ἄνθρωπος ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος αὐτοῦ), a messianic interpretation. The LXX also reads Γωγ (Gog) for the MT's אֲגַג (Agag), linking the oracle to Ezekiel's eschatology.
- **v. 8:** Both traditions preserve the wild ox imagery and the violent military metaphors, though the LXX intensifies "break bones" to "extract marrow."
- **v. 9:** The LXX preserves the echo of Genesis 49:9 and the Abrahamic blessing-curse formula, demonstrating the text's stability across traditions at this theologically critical juncture.
- **v. 14:** Both MT and LXX preserve the eschatological marker "in the latter days," confirming the prophetic orientation of the fourth oracle.
- **v. 17:** The LXX's rendering of שֵׁבֶט ("scepter") as ἄνθρωπος ("a man") makes the messianic interpretation more explicit than the MT. Both traditions preserve the star imagery, foundational for later messianic expectation.
- **v. 20:** Both traditions agree on Amalek's preeminence and coming destruction, preserving the ironic reversal.
- **v. 23-24:** The LXX follows the MT's sequence of oracles against the nations, though the syntax of the Kittim oracle varies slightly. The LXX preserves the geographic and military specificity of the predictions.
- **v. 25:** Both traditions record the abrupt departure of both Balaam and Balak, with no further interaction between them.

### Structural Observations

Numbers 24 completes the Balaam cycle with a dramatic escalation in both form and content. The chapter divides into five sections: (1) the narrative transition from divination to direct inspiration (vv. 1-2), (2) the third oracle celebrating Israel's beauty and power (vv. 3-9), (3) the confrontation between Balak and Balaam (vv. 10-14), (4) the fourth oracle with its messianic star-and-scepter prophecy (vv. 15-19), and (5) three brief oracles against surrounding nations (vv. 20-24), followed by the departure (v. 25).

The shift described in verse 1 is structurally pivotal. In chapters 22-23, Balaam employed the rituals of his trade -- altars, sacrifices, withdrawal to seek omens. Here he abandons these intermediaries entirely. The Spirit of God now comes upon him directly (v. 2), transforming him from a hired diviner into an ecstatic prophet. The elaborate self-identification in verses 3-4 (expanded in vv. 15-16) reflects this new prophetic self-consciousness.

The third oracle (vv. 3-9) is the most beautiful of the four, drawing on botanical and zoological imagery to depict Israel as a divinely planted paradise and an invincible predator. Its conclusion in verse 9 explicitly cites the Abrahamic covenant formula, connecting the oracle back to the foundational promises of Genesis. This citation in the mouth of a foreign seer validates the universal reach of the Abrahamic promises.

The fourth oracle (vv. 15-19) is the most theologically consequential. The addition of "knowledge of the Most High" in the introduction (v. 16) signals deeper revelation. The star-and-scepter prophecy of verse 17 generated an enormous interpretive tradition: the Septuagint's anthropological rendering, the Qumran community's eschatological reading (CD 7:18-20; 4QTestimonia), the Bar Kokhba identification, and early Christian messianic interpretation (Matthew 2:1-12; Revelation 22:16). The prophecy's referent moves from the proximate (David's conquests of Moab and Edom) to the eschatological horizon.

The brief oracles against Amalek (v. 20), the Kenites (vv. 21-22), and the maritime invaders (vv. 23-24) form a coda that extends the prophetic horizon beyond Israel and its immediate neighbors to encompass the great powers of the ancient world (Asshur, Kittim, Eber). The sequence ends on a note of universal upheaval -- "who shall live when God does this?" (v. 23) -- suggesting that the eschatological events set in motion by the star from Jacob will convulse the entire known world.

The mouth/word motif that governed chapters 22-23 reaches its culmination in the Balak-Balaam confrontation (vv. 10-14). Balak's fury and Balaam's appeal to the "mouth of YHWH" (v. 13) restate the central theological principle: the human mouth is subordinate to the divine word. Balaam cannot be bought because his mouth has been commandeered. The irony is complete when Balaam, having refused payment for blessing, voluntarily offers unsolicited prophecy about the eschatological future (v. 14) -- he now speaks not because he must but because the Spirit compels.
